Review conducted on perimeter firewalls only; internal firewalls and cloud security groups not reviewed
Reviews identify overly permissive rules but do not track remediation to completion
No metrics or trend analysis; each review is standalone without comparison to previous reviews
suggestedSources: Firewall rule analysis tool reports (Tufin, AlgoSec, FireMon)
Network security team review documentation
IT Risk Committee reporting packs showing review findings

evidenceItems.name: Firewall rule base audit reports
evidenceItems.description: Technical audit reports analysing the firewall rule base for compliance with policy, identifying rule base quality issues and security risks
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated rule base analysis report from firewall management tools identifying rule base issues by category
Identification of specific high-risk rules: rules allowing 'any' source or destination, rules allowing 'any' port, and rules with no hit count (unused)
Compliance scoring showing percentage of rules meeting policy requirements (specific source/destination, specific ports, documented justification)
Shadow rule analysis identifying rules that are never matched due to being overridden by higher-priority rules
Trend analysis showing rule base quality improving over successive review cycles
commonGaps: No automated rule base analysis; manual reviews of large rule bases are incomplete and error-prone
Audit reports identify issues but no risk rating or prioritisation of findings
Rule hit count analysis not performed; unused rules remain active indefinitely
Cloud security group rules not included in the audit scope
suggestedSources: Firewall management platform reports (Tufin, AlgoSec, FireMon)
Firewall vendor native rule analysis tools
External firewall audit reports from security consultants

evidenceItems.name: Remediation records for overly permissive rules
evidenceItems.description: Records documenting the identification, analysis, and remediation of overly permissive firewall rules
whatGoodLooksLike: Remediation tracker listing each overly permissive rule identified with original rule details, risk assessment, remediation plan, and current status
Evidence of rules being tightened from broad 'any' rules to specific source/destination/port combinations based on actual traffic analysis
Traffic analysis evidence (NetFlow, firewall logs) used to determine the minimum required access before tightening rules
Completion evidence showing before and after rule configurations for remediated rules
Metrics showing decreasing number of overly permissive rules over successive review cycles
commonGaps: Overly permissive rules identified in reviews but not remediated due to fear of causing service disruption
Remediation deferred indefinitely; same rules flagged in multiple review cycles without action
Rules tightened without traffic analysis, risking service disruption from overly aggressive rule changes
No tracking of remediation progress; findings from reviews not centrally managed
suggestedSources: Firewall change management records
Network security team remediation tracking
Firewall management platform rule change history

auditTips: Prepare evidence of at least two semi-annual rule reviews completed in the past 12 months with documented findings and remediation actions
Have the firewall rule base audit report ready showing the current state of rule base hygiene including percentage of rules with 'any' entries
Demonstrate the firewall change management process by providing examples of recent rule changes with business justification, security review, and approval evidence
Prepare metrics showing the trend of overly permissive rules being reduced over successive review cycles as evidence of active rule base management
Ensure all firewalls are accounted for in the review scope including perimeter firewalls, internal segmentation firewalls, and cloud security groups

10.39.title: Intrusion Detection & Prevention
10.39.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that IDS/IPS is deployed at all critical network points with current signatures, and that alerts are actively monitored and investigated rather than merely collected. They will look for evidence of timely triage and documented investigation outcomes.

evidenceItems.name: IDS/IPS deployment architecture
evidenceItems.description: Network architecture diagram showing IDS/IPS sensor placement at all critical network points including perimeter, DMZ, and internal segment boundaries
whatGoodLooksLike: Network diagram clearly marking IDS/IPS sensor locations at every critical network point
Sensor placement covers internet gateways, DMZ, core banking segments, and inter-VLAN boundaries
Document is version-controlled with last review date within 12 months
Coverage mapping shows alignment between critical asset register and sensor deployment
commonGaps: IDS/IPS deployed only at the perimeter with no coverage of internal east-west traffic
Architecture diagram outdated and does not reflect current network topology
No coverage of cloud or hybrid network segments
Sensor placement not aligned with critical asset locations
suggestedSources: Network architecture team (Visio/draw.io diagrams)
IDS/IPS management console (Snort, Suricata, Palo Alto, Fortinet)
IT asset register for critical network points

evidenceItems.name: Signature and rule update records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating regular and timely updates to IDS/IPS signatures and detection rules
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated signature update logs showing updates applied within 24 hours of vendor release
Change records for custom rules with documented justification
Validation testing records confirming updates do not disrupt legitimate traffic
No gaps exceeding 48 hours between signature releases and deployment
commonGaps: Signature updates are manual and applied inconsistently across sensors
No validation testing after signature updates leading to service disruptions
Subscription lapsed resulting in outdated signatures for extended periods
Custom rules not documented or reviewed
suggestedSources: IDS/IPS management console update logs
Vendor subscription portal (Cisco Talos, Emerging Threats, Fortiguard)
Change management system records

evidenceItems.name: Alert monitoring and investigation records
evidenceItems.description: Records of IDS/IPS alert monitoring, triage decisions, and investigation outcomes demonstrating timely response
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM dashboard showing real-time IDS/IPS alert monitoring with defined triage SLAs
Investigation records with timestamps showing response within defined SLA windows
Escalation records for high-severity alerts with management notification evidence
Monthly alert volume and investigation metrics reports
commonGaps: Alerts generated but no evidence of triage or investigation
High volume of uninvestigated alerts indicating alert fatigue
No defined SLAs for alert investigation and response
Investigation records lack detail on findings and remediation actions
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh, Microsoft Sentinel)
SOC ticketing system (ServiceNow, Jira)
Incident response records

evidenceItems.name: IDS/IPS policy and configuration standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy document defining IDS/IPS deployment standards, operational procedures, and configuration baselines
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy document with clear ownership, version control, and annual review sign-off
Defined deployment standards specifying sensor placement requirements
Configuration baselines for IDS/IPS sensors with hardening standards
Alert triage SLAs and escalation procedures clearly documented
commonGaps: Policy exists but has not been reviewed or updated for over 12 months
No configuration baseline standards leading to inconsistent sensor configurations
Policy does not define alert investigation SLAs or escalation procedures
No linkage between policy requirements and actual IDS/IPS configuration
suggestedSources: Information security policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
CISO office for policy sign-off records
IDS/IPS configuration management database

auditTips: Prepare a mapping document showing each critical network point and its corresponding IDS/IPS sensor with deployment evidence
Extract signature update logs for the past 12 months demonstrating consistent and timely updates across all sensors
Compile a sample of alert investigation records showing end-to-end triage from detection to resolution
Ensure the IDS/IPS policy has a current review date and is signed off by the CISO
Prepare metrics on alert volume, investigation rates, and mean time to investigate for the review period

10.40.title: Wireless Security
10.40.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that all wireless networks use WPA3 or equivalent encryption, that corporate and guest networks are properly segmented, that rogue access point detection is operational, and that regular wireless security assessments are conducted with findings remediated.

evidenceItems.name: Wireless security policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy defining wireless network security requirements including encryption, segmentation, and acceptable use
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly mandates WPA3 Enterprise or equivalent encryption for all wireless networks
Clear segmentation requirements separating corporate, guest, and IoT wireless networks
Rogue access point detection and response procedures defined
Policy reviewed and signed off within the past 12 months
commonGaps: Policy references outdated encryption standards such as WPA2 Personal or WEP
No explicit requirement for guest network isolation from internal networks
Policy does not address rogue access point detection or response
Policy not reviewed since initial publication
suggestedSources: Information security policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
CISO office for policy governance records
IT governance committee meeting minutes

evidenceItems.name: Wireless encryption and segmentation configuration
evidenceItems.description: Technical configuration evidence demonstrating WPA3 encryption enforcement and network segmentation between wireless and corporate networks
whatGoodLooksLike: Wireless controller configuration showing WPA3 Enterprise enforced on all SSIDs
VLAN and firewall rule configuration showing wireless-to-corporate segmentation
Guest network configuration demonstrating complete isolation from internal resources
Configuration change records showing transition from legacy protocols
commonGaps: Mixed encryption modes allowing fallback to WPA2 Personal or TKIP
Guest and corporate wireless on the same VLAN or subnet
No firewall rules between wireless and wired network segments
Legacy SSIDs still active with weaker encryption for compatibility
suggestedSources: Wireless LAN controller (Cisco WLC, Aruba Central, Meraki Dashboard)
Firewall configuration (Palo Alto, Fortinet, Cisco ASA)
Network management platform

evidenceItems.name: Rogue AP detection system deployment evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of wireless intrusion detection/prevention system deployment and operational rogue access point monitoring
whatGoodLooksLike: WIDS/WIPS deployment covering all premises with active monitoring enabled
Alert logs showing rogue AP detections with investigation and containment actions
Automated containment rules configured for detected rogue access points
Regular reports on rogue AP detection statistics and response times
commonGaps: Rogue AP detection not deployed in branch offices or remote sites
System deployed but alerts not monitored or investigated
No automated containment capability for detected rogue access points
Detection system not covering all frequency bands used by modern wireless devices
suggestedSources: WIDS/WIPS platform (Cisco CleanAir, Aruba RFProtect, AirMagnet)
Wireless controller rogue AP detection logs
SOC alert and investigation records

evidenceItems.name: Wireless security assessment reports
evidenceItems.description: Reports from regular wireless security assessments and penetration tests validating the effectiveness of wireless security controls
whatGoodLooksLike: Semi-annual wireless security assessment reports covering all office and branch locations
Assessment scope includes encryption validation, rogue AP scanning, and segmentation testing
Findings tracked with remediation timelines and closure evidence
Testing performed by qualified assessors with documented methodology
commonGaps: Assessments limited to headquarters with no coverage of branch premises
Assessment scope does not include segmentation validation or guest network testing
Findings identified but no remediation tracking or closure evidence
Assessments conducted more than 12 months ago
suggestedSources: Internal penetration testing team reports
Third-party wireless security assessment vendor reports
Remediation tracking system (Jira, ServiceNow)

auditTips: Prepare wireless controller configuration exports showing WPA3 enforcement across all SSIDs at every location
Compile VLAN and firewall rule evidence demonstrating guest network isolation from corporate resources
Extract rogue AP detection logs for the past 12 months with investigation and resolution records
Ensure wireless security assessment reports are current and cover all premises including branches
Prepare a remediation tracker showing closure of all wireless security assessment findings

10.41.title: Remote Access Security
10.41.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that remote access uses MFA and encrypted tunnels, that endpoint compliance is enforced before connection, that split tunnelling is disabled, and that all remote sessions are logged and actively monitored.

evidenceItems.name: Remote access security policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy defining security requirements for remote access including MFA, VPN encryption, endpoint compliance, and split tunnelling prohibition
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly prohibits split tunnelling and mandates MFA for all remote access
Endpoint compliance requirements clearly defined including minimum OS patch level and antivirus status
Policy covers all remote access methods including VPN, VDI, and remote desktop
Policy reviewed and approved by CISO within the past 12 months
commonGaps: Policy does not explicitly address split tunnelling prohibition
Endpoint compliance requirements vague or missing
Policy only covers VPN and does not address other remote access methods
No regular review cycle established
suggestedSources: Information security policy repository
CISO office for policy governance records
IT governance committee minutes

evidenceItems.name: VPN and MFA configuration evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical configuration evidence demonstrating VPN encryption settings and MFA enforcement for all remote access connections
whatGoodLooksLike: VPN gateway configuration showing AES-256 encryption with IKEv2 or TLS 1.3
MFA enforcement configuration showing hardware token or TOTP required for all VPN connections
Split tunnelling explicitly disabled in VPN profile configuration
Configuration aligned with CIS or vendor hardening benchmarks
commonGaps: VPN using legacy encryption protocols such as 3DES or SSL 3.0
MFA not enforced for all user groups or VPN profiles
Split tunnelling enabled for certain user groups without documented justification
No configuration baseline or hardening standards applied
suggestedSources: VPN gateway console (Cisco AnyConnect, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, Fortinet FortiClient)
MFA platform (Duo Security, RSA SecurID, Microsoft Authenticator)
Configuration management database

evidenceItems.name: Endpoint compliance check configuration
evidenceItems.description: Configuration evidence for endpoint compliance checks (NAC) that verify device health before granting remote access
whatGoodLooksLike: NAC policy configuration showing mandatory checks for OS patch level, antivirus status, and disk encryption
Non-compliant devices automatically quarantined or denied access
Compliance check results logged for audit trail purposes
Grace period and remediation workflow defined for non-compliant devices
commonGaps: Endpoint compliance checks not enforced or set to advisory mode only
Compliance checks limited to OS version without verifying patch status or antivirus
No quarantine or remediation workflow for non-compliant devices
BYOD devices exempted from compliance checks
suggestedSources: NAC platform (Cisco ISE, Aruba ClearPass, Forescout)
VPN client endpoint compliance module
Endpoint management platform (Microsoft Intune, JAMF)

evidenceItems.name: Remote access monitoring and logging evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of remote access session logging, monitoring, and investigation of anomalous activity
whatGoodLooksLike: VPN session logs capturing user identity, source IP, timestamp, duration, and bytes transferred
Anomaly detection rules configured for unusual remote access patterns such as off-hours access or concurrent sessions
Logs integrated into SIEM with correlation rules for remote access threats
Monthly reports on remote access usage patterns and security events
commonGaps: VPN logs not forwarded to SIEM for centralised monitoring
No anomaly detection rules for remote access sessions
Logs retained for less than three years
No regular review of remote access usage patterns
suggestedSources: VPN gateway logs
SIEM platform (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh)
SOC monitoring reports

auditTips: Export VPN gateway configuration showing encryption settings, MFA enforcement, and split tunnelling disabled for all profiles
Prepare NAC compliance check configuration showing mandatory health checks and quarantine rules
Extract VPN session logs for a sample period demonstrating logging completeness and SIEM integration
Compile evidence of quarterly remote access user list reviews with any access revocations documented
Ensure the remote access policy has a current review date and explicitly addresses all clause requirements

10.42.title: DNS Security
10.42.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that DNSSEC is implemented for authoritative domains, that DNS queries are logged and monitored for threats, that protections against DNS-based attacks are in place, and that DNS infrastructure is designed with redundancy and resilience.

evidenceItems.name: DNS security configuration documentation
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive documentation of DNS security configurations including threat protection, response policies, and access controls
whatGoodLooksLike: DNS security standards document covering DNSSEC, logging, threat protection, and redundancy
Configuration baselines for DNS servers aligned with CIS benchmarks
DNS firewall (RPZ) rules blocking known malicious domains with regular feed updates
Document version-controlled and reviewed within the past 12 months
commonGaps: No formal DNS security standards or configuration baselines documented
DNS servers using default configurations without hardening
No DNS firewall or threat feed integration
Documentation outdated and not reflecting current DNS architecture
suggestedSources: DNS platform administration (Infoblox, BlueCat, BIND, Microsoft DNS)
Network security team documentation
Configuration management database

evidenceItems.name: DNSSEC implementation evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating DNSSEC signing for all authoritative domains and DNSSEC validation on recursive resolvers
whatGoodLooksLike: DNSSEC signing enabled and validated for all institutional authoritative domains
Key management procedures documented including key rotation schedules
DNSSEC validation enabled on all recursive resolvers
External DNSSEC validation test results from tools such as DNSViz or Verisign Labs
commonGaps: DNSSEC not implemented for any institutional domains
DNSSEC signing enabled but key rotation not performed or documented
DNSSEC validation not enabled on internal recursive resolvers
No external validation testing to confirm DNSSEC is functioning correctly
suggestedSources: DNS hosting provider or registrar DNSSEC settings
DNSViz or Verisign DNSSEC Analyzer reports
Internal DNS resolver configuration

evidenceItems.name: DNS query logging and monitoring evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of DNS query logging, SIEM integration, and monitoring for suspicious DNS activity including tunnelling detection
whatGoodLooksLike: DNS query logs from all resolvers forwarded to SIEM in real time
Correlation rules configured for DNS tunnelling, DGA domain detection, and high-volume query anomalies
Monthly reports on DNS security events and threat detections
Logs retained for a minimum of three years
commonGaps: DNS query logging not enabled or only enabled on some resolvers
Logs not forwarded to SIEM for centralised monitoring
No detection rules for DNS tunnelling or domain generation algorithms
Log retention period insufficient for regulatory requirements
suggestedSources: DNS server query logs
SIEM platform (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh, Microsoft Sentinel)
DNS security analytics platform (Cisco Umbrella, Infoblox Threat Insight)

evidenceItems.name: DNS infrastructure redundancy design
evidenceItems.description: Architecture documentation demonstrating DNS infrastructure redundancy, geographic distribution, and failover capabilities
whatGoodLooksLike: DNS infrastructure design showing multiple authoritative servers in geographically separate locations
Anycast or load-balanced DNS with automatic failover documented and tested
Disaster recovery procedures for DNS infrastructure with tested RTO targets
Capacity planning evidence showing DNS infrastructure can handle peak loads and DDoS attacks
commonGaps: Single DNS server or single data centre deployment creating a single point of failure
No documented failover procedures or failover never tested
DNS infrastructure not included in disaster recovery planning
No DDoS protection for externally facing DNS infrastructure
suggestedSources: Network architecture team (infrastructure design documents)
DNS hosting provider SLA and redundancy documentation
Disaster recovery test records

auditTips: Run external DNSSEC validation checks (DNSViz, Verisign DNSSEC Analyzer) for all institutional domains and present the results
Extract DNS query logs from SIEM showing active monitoring and correlation rule outputs for a sample period
Prepare DNS infrastructure architecture diagrams showing redundancy and geographic distribution
Compile evidence of DNS threat protection including RPZ feeds, tunnelling detection, and blocked malicious domain statistics
Ensure DNS security standards documentation is current and covers all clause requirements

10.43.title: Network Monitoring
10.43.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that network monitoring operates 24/7 with adequate staffing, that flow data and deep packet inspection are utilised where appropriate, and that anomalous traffic patterns are detected and investigated promptly.

evidenceItems.name: Network monitoring architecture and tools
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the network monitoring architecture showing tool deployment, data collection points, and coverage across all network segments
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagram showing monitoring tool placement across all critical network segments
Flow data collection enabled on all core and distribution network devices
SIEM integration architecture showing centralised log aggregation and correlation
Tool inventory covering NMS, SIEM, flow analyser, and DPI capabilities
commonGaps: Monitoring limited to perimeter devices with no visibility into internal network segments
Flow data collection not enabled on internal switches and routers
No centralised SIEM integration resulting in siloed monitoring views
Cloud network segments not included in monitoring scope
suggestedSources: Network operations centre architecture documentation
SIEM platform deployment guide (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh)
Network flow analyser configuration (SolarWinds, Darktrace, ExtraHop)

evidenceItems.name: 24/7 monitoring procedures and staffing
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of 24/7 monitoring operations including shift schedules, staffing model, handover procedures, and escalation workflows
whatGoodLooksLike: SOC shift schedule showing 24/7 coverage with named analysts for each shift
Documented shift handover procedures with handover logs maintained
Escalation matrix defining response timelines for different alert severity levels
Training records showing analysts are certified and regularly upskilled
commonGaps: Monitoring relies on business-hours-only staff with no after-hours coverage
MSSP contract exists but no evidence of SLA monitoring or performance validation
No formal shift handover procedures resulting in dropped alerts during transitions
Insufficient analyst staffing leading to alert backlog during peak periods
suggestedSources: SOC operations manager for shift schedules and staffing records
MSSP contract and SLA documentation
HR records for SOC analyst training and certifications

evidenceItems.name: Network traffic analysis reports
evidenceItems.description: Reports from network traffic analysis showing baseline profiles, traffic trends, and anomaly investigations
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly traffic analysis reports showing baseline profiles and deviation trends
Anomaly investigation records with root cause analysis and resolution
Traffic pattern reports identifying new services, protocols, or communication flows
Bandwidth utilisation and capacity planning data derived from flow analysis
commonGaps: No baseline traffic profiles established for anomaly comparison
Traffic analysis limited to bandwidth monitoring without security context
Anomalies detected but not investigated or documented
Reports generated ad hoc rather than on a regular schedule
suggestedSources: Network flow analyser reports (SolarWinds NetFlow, Darktrace, ExtraHop)
SIEM traffic analysis dashboards
Network operations centre monthly reports

evidenceItems.name: Anomaly detection configuration and alert records
evidenceItems.description: Configuration evidence for anomaly detection rules and records of alerts generated, investigated, and resolved
whatGoodLooksLike: Anomaly detection rules configured for lateral movement, data exfiltration, and unusual protocol usage
Alert records showing timely investigation with documented triage decisions
Tuning records demonstrating false positive reduction without compromising detection
Detection coverage mapped against MITRE ATT&CK framework techniques
commonGaps: Anomaly detection not configured or using only default out-of-box rules
High false positive rate leading to alert fatigue and ignored alerts
No mapping of detection rules to known attack techniques or threat intelligence
Alert investigation records incomplete or missing resolution details
suggestedSources: SIEM correlation rule configuration
Network detection and response platform (Darktrace, Vectra, ExtraHop)
SOC ticketing system for alert investigation records

auditTips: Prepare SOC shift schedules and staffing evidence demonstrating continuous 24/7 monitoring coverage
Export SIEM dashboards showing network monitoring coverage across all critical segments
Compile a sample of anomaly detection alerts with full investigation and resolution records
Prepare network monitoring architecture diagrams showing tool deployment and data flow
Present monthly network monitoring reports for the past 12 months showing alert trends and investigation metrics

10.44.title: Backup Policy
10.44.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify the existence of a comprehensive backup policy covering scope, frequency, retention, and storage requirements. They will check that backups are encrypted, stored at geographically separate locations, and protected from ransomware attacks.

evidenceItems.name: Backup policy document
evidenceItems.description: Formal backup policy defining scope, frequency, retention periods, encryption, offsite storage, and ransomware protection requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy covers all critical systems with defined RPO and RTO targets for each tier
Encryption requirements specified for data at rest and in transit
Offsite storage requirements with minimum geographic separation defined
Ransomware protection measures including immutable storage explicitly addressed
commonGaps: Policy exists but does not specify encryption requirements for backup data
No ransomware-specific protection measures defined in the policy
RPO and RTO targets not defined or not aligned with business impact analysis
Policy not reviewed or updated for over 12 months
suggestedSources: IT governance policy repository
CISO office for policy sign-off records
Business continuity management office

evidenceItems.name: Backup schedule and scope definitions
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of backup schedules, scope coverage, and system-level backup configurations for all critical systems
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete inventory of systems in backup scope with classification tier and backup frequency
Automated backup schedules configured for daily, weekly, and monthly backups as per policy
Backup scope aligned with the critical asset register and BIA outputs
Retention settings configured per system tier matching policy requirements
commonGaps: Backup scope does not cover all systems identified as critical in the BIA
Newly deployed systems not added to the backup schedule in a timely manner
Backup frequency lower than required RPO targets for critical systems
No linkage between backup scope and the asset register or BIA
suggestedSources: Backup platform console (Veeam, Commvault, Veritas NetBackup, Rubrik)
CMDB or asset register for critical system inventory
Business impact analysis outputs

evidenceItems.name: Backup encryption and offsite storage evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating backup encryption configuration and geographically separate offsite storage
whatGoodLooksLike: Backup platform configuration showing AES-256 encryption enabled for all backup jobs
Encryption key management separate from backup storage with documented key rotation
Offsite replication logs showing successful data transfer to geographically separate location
Physical or logical separation evidence between primary and backup storage sites
commonGaps: Backup encryption not enabled for all backup jobs or repositories
Encryption keys stored on the same system as encrypted backups
Offsite storage in the same building or campus as primary data centre
No verification that offsite replicas are complete and restorable
suggestedSources: Backup platform encryption settings (Veeam, Commvault, Rubrik)
Key management system (Thales, HashiCorp Vault)
Offsite storage provider documentation or replication logs

evidenceItems.name: Ransomware protection measures for backups
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of ransomware protection measures including immutable storage, air-gapped copies, or write-once media
whatGoodLooksLike: Immutable backup repository configured with defined immutability lock period
Air-gapped backup copy maintained on isolated storage or tape media
Backup platform hardened against ransomware with restricted administrative access
Regular testing to verify immutable backups cannot be modified or deleted
commonGaps: No immutable or air-gapped backup copies maintained
Backup repositories accessible from the same network as production systems
Backup administrator credentials not protected with MFA or PAM
Ransomware resilience of backup infrastructure never tested
suggestedSources: Backup platform immutability settings (Veeam Hardened Repository, Rubrik)
Air-gapped tape or offline storage inventory
PAM system records for backup administrator access controls

auditTips: Prepare a mapping of all critical systems to their backup schedule, RPO/RTO targets, and current backup success rates
Export backup platform configuration showing encryption enabled for all repositories and backup jobs
Compile offsite replication logs demonstrating geographic separation and successful data transfer
Present immutable storage or air-gapped backup configuration evidence with testing records
Ensure the backup policy has a current review date and explicitly addresses ransomware protection

10.45.title: Backup Testing & Restoration
10.45.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that backup restoration tests are conducted quarterly for critical systems and annually for non-critical systems, that test results are documented with RTO/RPO measurements, and that any failures are remediated with root cause analysis.

evidenceItems.name: Backup restoration test schedule
evidenceItems.description: Documented schedule for backup restoration testing showing quarterly cadence for critical systems and annual cadence for non-critical systems
whatGoodLooksLike: Test schedule covers all systems in backup scope with defined testing frequency per tier
Critical systems scheduled for quarterly restoration tests throughout the year
Non-critical systems scheduled for at least annual restoration tests
Schedule approved by management and tracked for completion
commonGaps: No formal test schedule; testing performed ad hoc or only before audits
Critical systems not tested quarterly as required
Some systems in backup scope never included in restoration testing
Schedule exists but actual testing does not follow the schedule
suggestedSources: Infrastructure operations team test planning documents
Backup platform test scheduling configuration
IT operations calendar

evidenceItems.name: Restoration test results for critical systems (quarterly)
evidenceItems.description: Documented results of quarterly backup restoration tests for all critical systems showing data integrity, RTO/RPO measurements, and pass/fail status
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly test reports for each critical system with date, tester, and methodology
RTO and RPO measurements recorded and compared against target values
Data integrity validation results including checksums or record count verification
All four quarterly tests completed within the past 12 months
commonGaps: Fewer than four quarterly tests completed in the past 12 months
RTO/RPO not measured or not compared against target values
Data integrity not validated after restoration
Test results not formally documented or signed off
suggestedSources: Backup platform restoration test reports (Veeam, Commvault, Rubrik)
Infrastructure operations team test records
Change management system for test execution records

evidenceItems.name: Restoration test results for non-critical systems (annual)
evidenceItems.description: Documented results of annual backup restoration tests for non-critical systems with data integrity validation
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual test reports for each non-critical system completed within the past 12 months
Data integrity validation performed and documented for each test
RTO/RPO measurements recorded where applicable
Test coverage includes all non-critical systems in the backup scope
commonGaps: Non-critical systems not tested at all or tested more than 12 months ago
Only a sample of non-critical systems tested rather than full coverage
Data integrity not validated for non-critical system restorations
Test results not formally documented
suggestedSources: Backup platform restoration test reports
Infrastructure operations team annual test records
IT operations management reports

evidenceItems.name: Failure remediation records
evidenceItems.description: Records of remediation actions taken for any backup restoration test failures including root cause analysis and preventive measures
whatGoodLooksLike: Root cause analysis documented for each restoration test failure
Remediation actions assigned with responsible owners and target completion dates
Evidence of remediation completion and re-testing to confirm resolution
Preventive measures implemented to avoid recurrence of similar failures
commonGaps: Restoration test failures documented but no root cause analysis performed
Remediation actions identified but not tracked to completion
No re-testing after remediation to confirm the issue is resolved
Same failure types recurring across multiple test cycles without corrective action
suggestedSources: IT service management platform (ServiceNow, Jira)
Infrastructure operations team remediation records
IT risk register for backup-related risk entries

auditTips: Prepare a matrix showing all critical systems with their quarterly test dates, results, and RTO/RPO measurements for the past 12 months
Compile restoration test reports for non-critical systems demonstrating annual coverage
Extract failure remediation records showing root cause analysis, corrective actions, and re-test results
Ensure test results are formally documented with tester names, dates, and management sign-off
Prepare a trend analysis showing backup restoration success rates over time and improvement actions taken

10.46.title: Vendor Risk Assessment
10.46.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that comprehensive risk assessments are conducted for all technology vendors prior to engagement and on an ongoing basis, covering security posture, financial stability, regulatory compliance, and business continuity. Critical vendor reassessments must be completed at least annually.

evidenceItems.name: Vendor risk assessment framework and methodology
evidenceItems.description: Formal framework document defining the vendor risk assessment methodology, criteria, risk rating system, and assessment workflow
whatGoodLooksLike: Framework covers security, financial stability, regulatory compliance, and business continuity assessment domains
Standardised risk rating methodology with clear scoring criteria and thresholds
Defined approval workflows based on vendor risk tier
Framework reviewed and approved within the past 12 months
commonGaps: No formal framework; assessments performed ad hoc with inconsistent criteria
Assessment criteria do not cover all four required domains (security, financial, regulatory, continuity)
No standardised risk rating methodology leading to subjective assessments
Framework not reviewed or updated for changing regulatory requirements
suggestedSources: Vendor management office documentation
Enterprise risk management framework
Procurement and vendor onboarding procedures

evidenceItems.name: Completed vendor risk assessments
evidenceItems.description: Completed risk assessment reports for technology vendors covering all assessment domains with risk ratings and findings
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment reports available for all technology vendors with dates and assessor details
Each assessment covers security posture, financial stability, regulatory compliance, and business continuity
Risk ratings assigned using the standardised methodology with supporting evidence
Risk findings documented with vendor response and remediation commitments
commonGaps: Some technology vendors engaged without a pre-engagement risk assessment
Assessments limited to security only, omitting financial stability or continuity evaluation
Risk ratings assigned without adequate supporting evidence or documentation
Legacy vendors never assessed under the current framework
suggestedSources: Vendor management platform (Archer, ServiceNow VRM, OneTrust)
Procurement team vendor files
Vendor security questionnaire responses (SIG, CAIQ)

evidenceItems.name: Critical vendor annual reassessment records
evidenceItems.description: Records of annual risk reassessments for all critical technology vendors showing updated risk ratings and any changes in risk profile
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual reassessment completed for every vendor classified as critical
Updated risk ratings reflecting changes in vendor security posture or financial health
Comparison with previous assessment showing risk trend analysis
Reassessment includes review of vendor certifications and audit reports
commonGaps: Critical vendors not reassessed annually; reassessment only triggered by contract renewal
Reassessment is a superficial refresh rather than a comprehensive re-evaluation
No trend analysis comparing current assessment with previous results
Vendor certification and audit report expiry dates not tracked
suggestedSources: Vendor management platform reassessment records
Vendor-provided audit reports (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 certificates)
Financial health reports (Dun & Bradstreet, credit reports)

evidenceItems.name: Vendor risk rating and classification records
evidenceItems.description: Records showing risk classification of all technology vendors into risk tiers with documented rationale for each classification
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete vendor register with risk tier classification for every technology vendor
Classification criteria consistently applied across all vendors
Classification reviewed and updated at least annually or upon material changes
Clear escalation thresholds defining when vendor risk tier changes trigger management action
commonGaps: Vendor classifications inconsistent or based on subjective judgement
No documented rationale for risk tier assignments
Classifications not reviewed or updated when vendor scope changes
Critical vendor classification not aligned with BNM outsourcing guidelines
suggestedSources: Vendor management platform or register
Enterprise risk management system
Procurement system vendor records

auditTips: Prepare a complete vendor register showing all technology vendors with their risk tier classifications and most recent assessment dates
Compile completed risk assessment reports for a sample of vendors across all risk tiers
Present annual reassessment evidence for all vendors classified as critical, showing updated risk ratings
Ensure the vendor risk assessment framework is current and covers all four assessment domains required by the clause
Prepare evidence showing pre-engagement risk assessments for vendors onboarded in the past 12 months

10.47.title: Outsourcing Governance
10.47.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify the existence of a formal outsourcing governance framework with clear roles and oversight mechanisms, that board approval is obtained for material outsourcing, and that outsourced activities meet the same risk management and security standards as in-house operations.

evidenceItems.name: Outsourcing governance framework document
evidenceItems.description: Formal governance framework defining roles, responsibilities, approval authorities, and oversight mechanisms for technology outsourcing
whatGoodLooksLike: Framework defines governance structure with clear roles for business owners, IT, risk, and compliance
Materiality criteria defined for determining which outsourcing arrangements require board approval
Oversight mechanisms specified including reporting frequency, escalation procedures, and audit rights
Framework reviewed and approved by the board or board committee within the past 12 months
commonGaps: No formal governance framework; outsourcing managed ad hoc by individual departments
Framework does not define materiality criteria for board approval
Roles and responsibilities not clearly assigned or understood by stakeholders
Framework not updated to reflect current regulatory expectations or organisational changes
suggestedSources: Enterprise governance documentation repository
Board risk committee or IT committee charter
Vendor management office procedures

evidenceItems.name: Board approval records for material outsourcing
evidenceItems.description: Board meeting minutes and resolutions documenting approval of material technology outsourcing arrangements
whatGoodLooksLike: Board minutes recording discussion and approval of each material outsourcing arrangement
Supporting papers presented to the board including risk assessment and due diligence findings
Board resolution clearly recording the approval decision with conditions if applicable
All material outsourcing arrangements in force have corresponding board approval on record
commonGaps: Material outsourcing arrangements entered without board approval
Board papers do not include adequate risk assessment or due diligence information
Board approval obtained retrospectively after the outsourcing arrangement commenced
No clear definition of materiality leading to inconsistent escalation to the board
suggestedSources: Company secretary board meeting records
Board risk committee or IT committee meeting minutes
Board paper archive

evidenceItems.name: Outsourcing risk and security requirements
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of risk management and security standards applied to outsourced technology activities, demonstrating equivalence with internal standards
whatGoodLooksLike: Outsourcing security requirements documented and incorporated into vendor contracts
Requirements explicitly reference equivalence with internal security standards
Vendor compliance with security requirements verified through audits or assessments
Incident reporting requirements and timelines defined for outsourcing providers
commonGaps: Security requirements not specified in outsourcing contracts
Requirements lower than internal standards, creating a security gap
No mechanism to verify vendor compliance with security requirements
Incident reporting obligations not defined or not enforced
suggestedSources: Outsourcing contract security schedules
Vendor security requirements checklist
Vendor audit and compliance reports

evidenceItems.name: Outsourcing oversight and monitoring records
evidenceItems.description: Records of ongoing monitoring and oversight of outsourced technology activities including performance reviews, compliance checks, and risk assessments
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly oversight reports covering performance, security compliance, and risk profile for each material outsourcing arrangement
Regular service review meeting minutes with outsourcing providers
Escalation records for identified issues with tracked remediation
Annual comprehensive review of outsourcing arrangements reported to governance committees
commonGaps: No regular oversight reporting for outsourced activities
Oversight limited to SLA performance without covering security compliance
Service review meetings not documented or action items not tracked
Outsourcing arrangements not included in the institution's risk reporting framework
suggestedSources: Vendor management platform oversight reports
Service review meeting minutes and action logs
IT governance committee reports

auditTips: Prepare the outsourcing governance framework with evidence of board or committee approval and recent review date
Compile board minutes and resolutions for all material outsourcing arrangements currently in force
Present a mapping of outsourcing security requirements against internal standards to demonstrate equivalence
Prepare quarterly oversight reports for material outsourcing arrangements for the past 12 months
Ensure a complete register of all technology outsourcing arrangements is available with materiality classifications

10.48.title: SLA Monitoring
10.48.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that vendor SLA compliance is monitored on an ongoing basis, that breaches are documented and escalated through defined processes, and that persistent non-compliance triggers vendor review and potential contract renegotiation or termination.

evidenceItems.name: Vendor SLA monitoring reports
evidenceItems.description: Regular reports showing vendor performance against contractual SLA metrics with compliance status and trend analysis
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly SLA reports for all critical vendors covering availability, response time, and resolution time metrics
Automated measurement data rather than relying solely on vendor self-reporting
Trend analysis showing SLA performance over time with improvement or degradation patterns
Reports reviewed and signed off by vendor relationship owners
commonGaps: SLA monitoring reports produced only for some vendors or only when breaches occur
Metrics based entirely on vendor self-reporting without independent verification
No trend analysis to identify patterns of degrading performance
Reports not reviewed by management or no evidence of management action on findings
suggestedSources: IT service management platform (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy)
Vendor performance monitoring tools (SolarWinds, Datadog, PRTG)
Vendor-provided service reports

evidenceItems.name: SLA breach records and escalation evidence
evidenceItems.description: Records of SLA breaches with documentation of breach details, notification timelines, escalation actions, and vendor response
whatGoodLooksLike: Each SLA breach documented with timestamp, metric affected, magnitude of breach, and impact assessment
Breach notification to vendor within contractually defined timelines with acknowledgement evidence
Escalation to management for significant or repeated breaches with documented decisions
Vendor root cause analysis and corrective action plans obtained for each breach
commonGaps: SLA breaches not formally documented or tracked
No evidence of breach notification to the vendor or escalation to management
Vendor not required to provide root cause analysis for breaches
Breach records incomplete with missing impact assessments or resolution details
suggestedSources: IT service management platform incident and problem records
Vendor correspondence and breach notification emails
Vendor management office escalation records

evidenceItems.name: Remediation plans for SLA non-compliance
evidenceItems.description: Documented remediation plans from vendors addressing SLA non-compliance with timelines, milestones, and progress tracking
whatGoodLooksLike: Vendor-submitted remediation plans with specific actions, responsible parties, and target completion dates
Progress tracking showing milestone completion against plan
Institution's review and acceptance of remediation plans
Re-testing or validation evidence confirming remediation effectiveness
commonGaps: No formal remediation plans requested from vendors for SLA breaches
Remediation plans accepted but not tracked to completion
No validation that remediation actions actually resolved the SLA performance issue
Same SLA breaches recurring despite remediation plans
suggestedSources: Vendor management office remediation tracking records
Vendor correspondence and remediation plan submissions
IT service management platform problem records

evidenceItems.name: Vendor performance review records
evidenceItems.description: Records of formal vendor performance review meetings covering SLA achievement, breach history, remediation effectiveness, and overall relationship assessment
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly performance review meeting minutes for all critical vendors
Performance scorecards covering SLA metrics, incident volumes, and overall satisfaction
Action items from reviews tracked to completion
Evidence of contract renegotiation or termination discussions for persistently non-compliant vendors
commonGaps: No formal performance review meetings conducted with vendors
Performance reviews limited to annual contract renewal discussions
No performance scorecard or structured assessment methodology
Persistent SLA non-compliance not escalated to contract renegotiation or termination discussions
suggestedSources: Vendor management office meeting minutes and scorecards
Contract management system
Procurement team vendor performance records

auditTips: Prepare monthly SLA monitoring reports for all critical vendors for the past 12 months with trend analysis
Compile SLA breach records showing documented escalation and remediation for each breach
Present vendor performance review meeting minutes for critical vendors demonstrating quarterly cadence
Ensure evidence of contract renegotiation or termination discussions for any vendor with persistent non-compliance
Prepare a summary showing how SLA metrics are measured independently rather than relying solely on vendor self-reporting

10.49.title: Vendor Exit Strategy
10.49.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that documented exit strategies exist for all critical technology vendors, covering data migration, service transition, operational continuity, and secure data destruction. They will check that exit plans are reviewed or tested at least annually.

evidenceItems.name: Vendor exit strategy documents
evidenceItems.description: Documented exit strategies for all critical technology vendors detailing transition procedures, timelines, and resource requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Exit strategy documents available for every vendor classified as critical
Each strategy covers data migration, service transition, continuity, and data destruction
Timelines, resource estimates, and responsible parties clearly defined
Strategies updated within the past 12 months to reflect current arrangements
commonGaps: No exit strategies documented for some or all critical vendors
Exit strategies are generic templates without vendor-specific detail
Strategies do not address data migration complexity or timelines realistically
Exit strategies not updated after significant changes to vendor arrangements
suggestedSources: Vendor management office exit planning documents
Business continuity management office
IT architecture team for alternative solution assessments

evidenceItems.name: Data migration and transition plans
evidenceItems.description: Detailed technical plans for migrating data from each critical vendor and transitioning services to alternative providers
whatGoodLooksLike: Data migration plans specify data formats, transfer mechanisms, and validation procedures
Alternative service providers or in-house options identified and assessed
Parallel running requirements and cutover criteria defined
Migration timelines aligned with contractual notice periods and transition assistance provisions
commonGaps: No detailed data migration plans; only high-level statements of intent
Alternative service providers not identified or assessed
No parallel running or cutover procedures defined
Data export formats not validated or tested with the current vendor
suggestedSources: IT architecture team migration planning documents
Vendor contract data portability clauses
Previous vendor migration project records

evidenceItems.name: Secure data destruction requirements
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of data destruction requirements for exiting vendors including destruction standards, certification, and verification procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Data destruction standards specified (e.g., NIST 800-88, DoD 5220.22-M) in vendor contracts
Vendor required to provide a certificate of destruction upon completion
Verification procedures defined for confirming data destruction completeness
Timeframes for data destruction after contract termination clearly specified
commonGaps: No data destruction requirements specified in vendor contracts
Destruction standards not defined or left to vendor discretion
No certificate of destruction required or obtained from vendors
No verification procedure to confirm data has been destroyed
suggestedSources: Vendor contract data destruction clauses
Information security data destruction standards
Previous vendor exit data destruction certificates

evidenceItems.name: Annual exit plan review or test records
evidenceItems.description: Records of annual reviews or tests of vendor exit strategies validating feasibility and identifying gaps
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual review records for each critical vendor exit strategy with reviewer and date
Review findings documenting gaps, dependencies, and update requirements
Tabletop exercise or simulation records for high-criticality vendor exits
Remediation actions for identified gaps tracked to completion
commonGaps: Exit strategies never reviewed or tested since initial creation
Reviews are superficial check-the-box exercises without meaningful analysis
No tabletop exercises or simulations conducted for the most critical vendor exits
Gaps identified in reviews but not remediated
suggestedSources: Vendor management office annual review records
Business continuity management tabletop exercise records
IT risk committee meeting minutes

auditTips: Prepare a register of all critical vendors with their exit strategy document references and last review dates
Compile exit strategy documents for all critical vendors demonstrating vendor-specific detail rather than generic templates
Present contractual provisions showing data return, transition assistance, and data destruction obligations
Provide annual review or tabletop exercise records demonstrating exit plan validation
Ensure data destruction requirements are explicitly defined in vendor contracts and aligned with recognised standards

10.50.title: Cloud Risk Assessment
10.50.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that comprehensive risk assessments are conducted before adopting any cloud service, covering data sovereignty, regulatory compliance, security controls, vendor lock-in, and the shared responsibility model. They will check that assessments are reviewed upon material changes.

evidenceItems.name: Cloud risk assessment methodology
evidenceItems.description: Formal methodology document defining the approach, criteria, and workflow for assessing risks of cloud service adoption
whatGoodLooksLike: Methodology covers all five required domains: data sovereignty, regulatory compliance, security, vendor lock-in, and shared responsibility
Risk rating criteria defined with clear thresholds for acceptance, conditional approval, and rejection
Approval workflow defined including CISO and management sign-off requirements
Reassessment triggers clearly specified for material changes
commonGaps: No formal methodology; cloud risk assessments conducted ad hoc with varying criteria
Assessment criteria do not cover all five required domains
No defined approval workflow or risk acceptance authority
Reassessment triggers not specified for material changes
suggestedSources: Cloud governance framework documentation
Enterprise risk management methodology
CISO office cloud security standards

evidenceItems.name: Completed cloud risk assessments
evidenceItems.description: Completed risk assessment reports for all cloud services in use, covering the five required assessment domains with risk ratings and findings
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessment completed for every cloud service before adoption commenced
Each assessment covers data sovereignty, regulatory compliance, security controls, vendor lock-in, and shared responsibility
Risk findings documented with treatment plans and residual risk acceptance
Management sign-off obtained for each assessment
commonGaps: Cloud services adopted without a pre-adoption risk assessment
Assessments incomplete or missing coverage of data sovereignty or vendor lock-in
Risk findings identified but no treatment plans or acceptance decisions documented
Shadow IT cloud services in use without any risk assessment
suggestedSources: Cloud governance platform or risk register
CISO office cloud assessment records
Cloud provider compliance portal (AWS Artifact, Azure Trust Center, Google Cloud Compliance)

evidenceItems.name: Shared responsibility model documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation clearly delineating security responsibilities between the institution and each cloud provider for every cloud service in use
whatGoodLooksLike: Shared responsibility matrix for each cloud service showing institutional vs provider responsibilities
Responsibilities mapped against specific security controls (encryption, patching, access control, monitoring)
Institution's responsibilities documented with assigned owners and implementation evidence
Model reviewed and updated when cloud service tier or scope changes
commonGaps: No documented shared responsibility model; reliance on generic provider documentation
Institution's responsibilities not clearly assigned to specific teams or individuals
Shared responsibility gaps where neither party has assumed responsibility for certain controls
Model not updated when cloud service configuration changes
suggestedSources: Cloud provider shared responsibility documentation
Internal cloud security standards
Cloud architect team documentation

evidenceItems.name: Risk assessment review records for material changes
evidenceItems.description: Records of risk assessment reviews triggered by material changes to cloud arrangements, showing updated risk ratings and treatment plans
whatGoodLooksLike: Change records triggering reassessment with description of material change
Updated risk assessment reflecting the impact of the change
Revised risk ratings and treatment plans where applicable
Management sign-off on updated assessment
commonGaps: Material changes to cloud arrangements not triggering reassessment
No defined criteria for what constitutes a material change
Reassessments not documented or not updating risk ratings
Original assessment never revisited despite significant changes to cloud usage
suggestedSources: Change management system records
Cloud governance platform reassessment records
CISO office cloud risk review documentation

auditTips: Prepare a register of all cloud services in use with their risk assessment dates and current risk ratings
Compile completed cloud risk assessments demonstrating coverage of all five required domains
Present shared responsibility matrices for each cloud service with assigned institutional owners
Provide evidence of risk assessment reviews triggered by material changes in the past 12 months
Ensure cloud risk assessment methodology is current and covers BNM-specific requirements for data sovereignty and regulatory compliance

10.51.title: Access Control Policy
10.51.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will look for a formally documented access control policy that defines rules and rights for all users and systems, has been reviewed within the past 12 months, and has been communicated to all staff. They will verify the policy scope covers all systems including cloud environments.

evidenceItems.name: Access control policy document with version and effective date
evidenceItems.description: Formal access control policy document with version control, effective date, and management approval defining access rules for all systems and information assets
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy document with clear version number, effective date, and CISO or board-level approval
Comprehensive coverage of access control principles including least privilege, need-to-know, and segregation of duties
Scope explicitly covers all systems including cloud, third-party, and mobile applications
Policy includes sections on user provisioning, de-provisioning, access reviews, and privileged access
commonGaps: Policy document exists but lacks version control or effective date
Scope does not explicitly include cloud environments or third-party systems
Policy too high-level without actionable requirements for different system types
Policy does not address privileged access management or service account controls
suggestedSources: Information security policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
CISO office for policy governance records
Document management system with version history

evidenceItems.name: Annual policy review record or sign-off
evidenceItems.description: Records of the most recent annual review of the access control policy including review participants, changes made, and management approval
whatGoodLooksLike: Review record showing date, participants, and changes made during the annual review
Comparison document showing changes between previous and current policy versions
CISO or equivalent authority sign-off on the reviewed policy
Review conducted within the past 12 months
commonGaps: No evidence of annual review; policy last reviewed more than 12 months ago
Review performed but not formally documented with participants and changes
Policy reviewed but not formally approved or signed off by appropriate authority
Review did not consider changes to the operating environment or regulatory requirements
suggestedSources: Policy governance records and review tracking
IT governance committee meeting minutes
Document management system version history

evidenceItems.name: List of systems and information assets in scope
evidenceItems.description: Inventory of all systems and information assets covered by the access control policy, including classification and access control mechanism used
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete inventory covering on-premises systems, cloud services, and third-party applications
Each system mapped to its access control mechanism (Active Directory, IAM platform, local accounts)
Systems classified by criticality and data sensitivity levels
Inventory reviewed and updated at least quarterly
commonGaps: Inventory incomplete; newly deployed systems or shadow IT not included
Cloud services not included in the asset inventory
No classification of systems by criticality or data sensitivity
Inventory not updated regularly leading to stale records
suggestedSources: CMDB or IT asset register
Cloud service inventory or CASB discovery reports
Application portfolio management system

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of communication to staff
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the access control policy has been communicated to all staff and their understanding confirmed through acknowledgement or awareness training
whatGoodLooksLike: Email or intranet communication records showing policy distribution to all staff
Staff acknowledgement records confirming receipt and understanding of the policy
Security awareness training records covering access control policy requirements
High completion rate (above 95%) for policy acknowledgement or training
commonGaps: No evidence of policy communication to staff
Communication sent but no acknowledgement tracking mechanism
Low completion rates for policy acknowledgement or awareness training
Communication limited to IT staff and not extended to all employees
suggestedSources: Learning management system (LMS) training records
Email distribution records or intranet publishing logs
HR onboarding documentation for new staff

auditTips: Ensure the access control policy document has a current effective date and CISO sign-off within the past 12 months
Prepare annual review records showing the review process, participants, and changes made
Compile the system and asset inventory demonstrating comprehensive scope including cloud services
Present staff acknowledgement completion rates and evidence of policy communication
Verify the policy explicitly covers cloud environments, third-party access, and privileged access management

10.52.title: Cloud Data Residency
10.52.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that data stored in cloud environments complies with applicable data residency and sovereignty requirements, that data classification determines permissible locations, and that the institution maintains visibility over the physical location of its data at all times.

evidenceItems.name: Cloud data residency policy
evidenceItems.description: Formal policy defining data residency requirements for cloud-hosted data including approved jurisdictions by data classification tier
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy specifies approved data storage locations by data classification tier
Malaysian data residency requirements explicitly addressed for sensitive and restricted data
Policy aligns with BNM outsourcing and data governance guidelines
Exceptions process defined with management approval requirements
commonGaps: No formal data residency policy for cloud environments
Policy does not differentiate requirements by data classification
Malaysian-specific residency requirements not explicitly addressed
No exceptions process defined for data that must be stored outside approved locations
suggestedSources: Information security policy repository
Cloud governance framework documentation
Compliance team regulatory requirements register

evidenceItems.name: Data classification and location mapping
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive mapping of data classifications to their current cloud storage and processing locations
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete mapping covering all data assets stored in cloud environments
Each data asset classified and mapped to its physical storage region
Mapping verified against cloud provider configuration and monitoring data
Updated at least quarterly and upon new cloud deployments
commonGaps: No comprehensive mapping of data to physical locations
Data classification not applied to cloud-hosted data
Mapping based on assumptions rather than verified location data
Mapping not updated when cloud service configurations change
suggestedSources: Data classification register
Cloud provider region configuration (AWS Region settings, Azure Geography)
Cloud security posture management (CSPM) tool reports

evidenceItems.name: Cloud provider data residency agreements
evidenceItems.description: Contractual agreements with cloud providers confirming data residency commitments and restrictions on cross-border data transfers
whatGoodLooksLike: Cloud provider contracts include explicit data residency commitments specifying storage regions
Contractual prohibition on data transfer to non-approved jurisdictions
Provider notification obligations for any changes to data storage locations
Data residency commitments cover all data types including backups, logs, and metadata
commonGaps: No data residency clauses in cloud provider contracts
Contracts only address primary data location but not backups or metadata
No notification obligation for the provider if data locations change
Provider terms allow data movement between regions for operational reasons
suggestedSources: Cloud provider contracts and data processing agreements
Legal team contract archives
Cloud provider compliance portal (AWS Artifact, Azure Trust Center)

evidenceItems.name: Data location monitoring evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of continuous monitoring of cloud data locations to verify compliance with residency requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Cloud configuration monitoring showing region-locked deployments
CSPM or cloud-native tool reports verifying data location compliance
Alerts configured for any data location policy violations
Quarterly compliance reports confirming data remains within approved locations
commonGaps: No monitoring of actual data locations in cloud environments
Reliance on initial configuration without ongoing verification
No alerting for data location policy violations
Monitoring does not cover all cloud services or data types
suggestedSources: CSPM platform (Prisma Cloud, Wiz, Microsoft Defender for Cloud)
Cloud provider configuration audit tools (AWS Config, Azure Policy)
Cloud governance team compliance reports

auditTips: Prepare the data classification and location mapping showing all cloud data assets with their physical storage regions
Export cloud provider configuration evidence showing region-locking for all critical cloud services
Compile cloud provider contractual provisions addressing data residency commitments
Present CSPM or cloud-native monitoring evidence verifying ongoing data location compliance
Ensure the data residency policy explicitly addresses Malaysian regulatory requirements for financial institution data

10.53.title: User Access Provisioning
10.53.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that formal provisioning and de-provisioning processes exist following least privilege, that access is granted with documented owner approval, and that de-provisioning occurs within 24 hours of employment termination. They will sample recent terminations to verify compliance.

evidenceItems.name: User access provisioning procedure
evidenceItems.description: Formal procedure document defining the end-to-end user access provisioning lifecycle including request, approval, implementation, and review processes
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure covers joiners, movers, and leavers with defined workflows for each scenario
Approval requirements clearly defined specifying data or system owner as the approver
Least privilege principle explicitly referenced as the basis for all provisioning decisions
Procedure includes service account and third-party access provisioning
commonGaps: No formal provisioning procedure; access granted via informal email requests
Procedure does not specify who must approve access requests
Least privilege not enforced; users receive broad access profiles by default
Service accounts and third-party access not covered by the provisioning procedure
suggestedSources: IAM team procedure documentation
IT service management process library
Information security standards and procedures

evidenceItems.name: Access request and approval records
evidenceItems.description: Records of user access requests with documented approvals from system or data owners demonstrating formal provisioning
whatGoodLooksLike: Access requests captured in a ticketing or IAM system with timestamps and requester details
Approval from the designated system or data owner recorded with approver identity and timestamp
Access granted matches the approved request without scope creep
Complete audit trail from request through approval to provisioning
commonGaps: Access requests handled via email with no centralised tracking
Approvals not obtained from the designated system or data owner
No audit trail linking the access request to the actual provisioning action
Bulk access grants without individual approval for each user
suggestedSources: IAM platform (SailPoint, Saviynt, Microsoft Entra ID Governance)
IT service management platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
Active Directory or application access logs

evidenceItems.name: De-provisioning procedure and compliance records
evidenceItems.description: De-provisioning procedure and compliance records demonstrating access revocation within 24 hours of employment termination
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated de-provisioning workflow triggered by HR system termination events
De-provisioning covers all systems including AD, email, VPN, applications, and cloud services
Compliance dashboard showing percentage of terminations meeting the 24-hour SLA
Sample records showing timestamps of termination event and access revocation within 24 hours
commonGaps: De-provisioning is manual and not triggered by HR system integration
Access revoked from some systems but not all, leaving residual access
De-provisioning routinely exceeds the 24-hour SLA without escalation
No monitoring or reporting on de-provisioning compliance rates
suggestedSources: IAM platform de-provisioning logs
HR system termination records cross-referenced with AD disable dates
IT service management platform leaver tickets

evidenceItems.name: Least privilege configuration evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating least privilege enforcement through role-based access control and access restriction configurations
whatGoodLooksLike: RBAC role definitions documented with mapped permissions for each role
Users assigned to roles matching their job function with no excessive permissions
Segregation of duties controls preventing conflicting access combinations
Regular review of role definitions and user-to-role assignments
commonGaps: No RBAC implemented; access granted on a per-user basis without role standardisation
Roles defined but overly broad, granting more access than required
No segregation of duties controls implemented
Role definitions not reviewed or updated when job functions change
suggestedSources: IAM platform role configuration
Active Directory group membership reports
Application access control configuration

auditTips: Prepare a sample of recent access provisioning requests showing end-to-end workflow from request through owner approval to provisioning
Extract de-provisioning compliance data showing the percentage of terminations meeting the 24-hour SLA for the past 12 months
Compile a cross-reference of recent employee terminations against AD account disable dates to demonstrate compliance
Present RBAC role definitions and evidence of least privilege enforcement across critical systems
Ensure the provisioning procedure is current and covers all access scenarios including joiners, movers, leavers, and third parties

10.54.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
10.54.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that MFA is enforced for all privileged access to critical systems, that the authentication mechanism is resistant to replay and phishing attacks, and that SMS OTP is not used for privileged access. They will check MFA configuration evidence and coverage across all critical systems.

evidenceItems.name: PAM policy document (version + date)
evidenceItems.description: Formal MFA and privileged access management policy mandating multi-factor authentication for all privileged access and prohibiting SMS OTP
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly mandates MFA for all privileged access to critical systems, networks, and applications
SMS OTP explicitly prohibited for privileged access with approved alternatives listed
Policy specifies phishing-resistant MFA methods (FIDO2, hardware tokens, TOTP)
Policy reviewed and approved within the past 12 months with version control
commonGaps: Policy does not explicitly prohibit SMS OTP for privileged access
MFA requirement stated but no specification of acceptable authentication methods
Policy does not cover third-party privileged access to critical systems
Policy not reviewed or updated for over 12 months
suggestedSources: Information security policy repository
CISO office for policy governance records
PAM system documentation

evidenceItems.name: Asset register confirming critical system scope
evidenceItems.description: Register of all critical systems, networks, and applications within scope of the MFA requirement for privileged access
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete register of critical systems aligned with the institution's asset classification
Each system mapped to its MFA implementation status and method used
MFA coverage percentage tracked and reported with target of 100% for critical systems
Register reviewed at least quarterly and updated upon new system deployments
commonGaps: No formal critical system register linked to MFA requirements
Some critical systems not identified or excluded from MFA scope
MFA implementation status not tracked against the critical system register
Register not updated when new critical systems are deployed
suggestedSources: CMDB or IT asset register
IAM platform MFA enrollment reports
Business impact analysis critical system list

evidenceItems.name: MFA configuration screenshots or audit logs
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating MFA configuration and enforcement on critical systems, showing that MFA cannot be bypassed
whatGoodLooksLike: Configuration screenshots from each critical system showing MFA enforcement enabled
Conditional access policies requiring MFA for all privileged roles
Audit logs showing MFA verification for privileged access sessions
No legacy or fallback authentication methods that bypass MFA
commonGaps: MFA configured but not enforced, allowing users to skip MFA enrollment
Some critical systems do not support MFA integration and lack compensating controls
Legacy authentication protocols enabled that bypass MFA requirements
MFA enforcement inconsistent across different critical systems
suggestedSources: MFA platform configuration (Duo Security, RSA SecurID, Microsoft Entra ID)
PAM system (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea)
Critical system authentication configuration

evidenceItems.name: Break-glass procedure with MFA controls
